questions.question: In an immersion test the sound must cross a water path before entering the part. Which wave mode can make that crossing?
questions.explanation: Liquids support compression but cannot sustain shear stress, so only longitudinal waves propagate through water; shear, Rayleigh, and Lamb modes need a solid.

questions.question: Rank the wave modes in steel from fastest to slowest:
options: All three travel at the same velocity
questions.explanation: In steel: longitudinal ≈ 5,920 m/s, shear ≈ 3,240 m/s (about half), and Rayleigh ≈ 2,900 m/s (about 90% of shear).

questions.question: An angle-beam wedge produces a shear wave in the test part primarily by:
options: Refraction and mode conversion of the incident longitudinal wave at the wedge-to-part interface
questions.explanation: The element generates a longitudinal wave in the wedge; entering the metal obliquely between the critical angles, that energy refracts and mode-converts so only a shear wave continues in the part.

questions.question: A 1 MHz shear wave travels through steel (3,240 m/s = 3.24 mm/µs). Its wavelength is:
questions.explanation: λ = v ÷ f = 3.24 mm/µs ÷ 1 MHz = 3.24 mm. The 5.92 mm distractor uses the longitudinal velocity.

questions.question: The two mechanisms of material attenuation are:
questions.explanation: Absorption converts vibration to heat through internal friction; scattering deflects energy at grain boundaries, pores, and inclusions. Beam spread thins the beam too, but it is geometry, not material loss.

questions.question: Sound passes from steel (5,920 m/s) into water (1,480 m/s) without changing frequency. Its wavelength:
questions.explanation: λ = v ÷ f and frequency is fixed by the source, so wavelength scales with velocity: 1,480 ÷ 5,920 = ¼ of the wavelength in steel.

questions.question: Water (Z = 1.48 MRayl) couples sound into aluminum (Z = 17.1 MRayl). About what fraction of the energy reflects at the boundary?
questions.explanation: R = ((17.1 − 1.48) ÷ (17.1 + 1.48))² = (15.6 ÷ 18.6)² ≈ 0.71, so about 71% reflects and 29% transmits into the aluminum.

questions.question: A tight fatigue crack reflects nearly all of the sound that strikes it because:
options: Crack faces are always smooth and polished
The gas film inside the crack creates an extreme impedance mismatch with the surrounding metal
Cracks always lie parallel to the beam
The crack tip focuses the beam back to the probe
questions.explanation: Even the tightest crack carries a gas layer, and a metal-to-gas boundary reflects essentially 100% of the energy — the same physics that makes the back wall echo.

questions.question: A 36.6° incident beam in an acrylic wedge (2,730 m/s) enters steel (shear velocity 3,240 m/s; sin 36.6° = 0.596). The refracted shear angle is about:
questions.explanation: Snell: sin θ₂ = 0.596 × (3,240 ÷ 2,730) = 0.596 × 1.187 = 0.708, and the angle whose sine is 0.708 is about 45° — this is exactly how a 45° wedge is designed.

questions.question: Doubling the element diameter of a probe at the same frequency does what to the sound beam?
options: Near field becomes four times longer and the beam spread half-angle gets smaller
Near field becomes twice as long and the beam spreads more
Near field shrinks to one quarter and the beam gets narrower
No change — beam shape depends only on frequency
questions.explanation: N = D²f ÷ 4v grows with the square of diameter (×4), while sin γ = 1.22v ÷ (Df) shrinks as diameter grows — a bigger element throws a longer near field but a tighter, straighter beam.
